narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 42, Year 1 school decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 2, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time—details the admissions model carefully reviewed alongside thousands of similar student profiles to predict bar exam success. The model estimates a 52% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass and graduate strongly. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your background, with your LSAT score standing out as the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward passing because your 42 is well above the typical 36.7 average. However, your Year 3 school decile of 2—lower than the average 5.53—had the next biggest impact, pulling strongly toward a failure prediction by signaling a dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, below the usual 3.21, added further weight in the same concerning direction, though its influence was smaller than the others. Overall, while your standout LSAT gave you a boost many applicants lack, the model saw your weaker later grades and undergrad record as bigger risks for bar struggles, outweighing strengths like your full-time commitment, which is common among 93% of students. You're not far off—your profile shows real potential, especially that LSAT edge that sets you apart from the average. To turn this around, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, or focus on boosting recent academic deciles through grad courses or targeted prep that mirrors bar demands. Many in similar situations, including Hispanic students who've faced early hurdles, have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. Reach out to advisors for bar passage strategies tailored to your background, and reapply after showing upward trends—schools value resilience like yours. This isn't the end; it's a clear map to get stronger.
